turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Discussing Study or Career Plans with an Advisor

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: “Hi, I'm about to graduate and I'm unsure whether to study further or start working. Yeah, I don't know what I can do now. I don't know if I have to continue with my studying or I should start my start to work now. Yeah, I really need the advice now.”

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: I understand this is a big decision. Could you tell me more about your field of study and what kind of career interests you? That will help me provide more specific advice.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: “I'm studying Virtual Art Business with a major in International Business. I don't know. I don't know my favorite career.”

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: International Business offers a lot of exciting career paths! If you're considering working right after graduation, you might explore roles in multinational companies, trade organizations, or export/import businesses. Alternatively, if you’re considering further studies, you could look into specialized programs like an MBA or a master's in international relations. What excites you more—starting your career now or pursuing further studies?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: “I have seen that I also have a diploma degree, and it's just one year studying, and, yes, should I do a diploma degree or should I start working? I know. Because I also plan that I want to have a master's degree, and after working, after three years or four years, I don't know what I'm saying now.”

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: It sounds like you're considering a few options. If you’re interested in gaining practical experience first, starting to work after graduation can provide valuable insights into the industry. Then, pursuing a master's degree later on could enhance your expertise and open up advanced career opportunities. Alternatively, if you feel ready and passionate about further studies, the diploma could be a great way to specialize even more. It ultimately depends on your personal goals and readiness. How do you feel about gaining work experience first?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: “I know, I know, yeah, I'm sorry, um, I don't know what should I do now, I have no idea with that.”

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: It’s completely normal to feel uncertain. Take your time to weigh your options. If you need more guidance, consider speaking with professionals in the field or seeking internships to get a feel for the work environment. Also, consider reaching out to alumni or career counselors for insights. Remember, whichever path you choose, you can always adjust and find your way. If you need more help or want to discuss further, I’m here for you!

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: “Do I need to study overseas, like having a master's degree overseas? I think that I can have more challenges and I can have more experiences like talking with other people, talking, helping others, yeah, I don't need things, and yeah, I don't know, like...”

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: It sounds like you're considering the value of gaining practical experience and expanding your network. Both are important aspects of career growth. If you decide to start working now, you can still pursue a master's degree later to further enhance your skills and opportunities. Take your time to reflect on what aligns best with your personal and professional goals. If you have any other questions or need more guidance, I’m here to help!

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: “So, well, if I want to study abroad, how can I prepare now? Like, then, like, having the undergraduate, I'm sorry, having the graduate degree and going other, going other, another country and having, and doing master degree in that country and having a job in, in that country, is it, is it, is it good or not?”

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Studying abroad can be a wonderful experience, offering exposure to different cultures and perspectives. Here’s how you can prepare: Research: Look into universities and programs that align with your career goals and interests. Application: Prepare your academic transcripts, letters of recommendation, and any required test scores (like TOEFL or IELTS). Finances: Plan your budget, considering tuition, living expenses, and travel costs. Look into scholarships or financial aid. Cultural Preparation: Learn about the culture and language of the country you’ll be studying in. Networking: Connect with alumni or current students to gain insights and advice. Studying abroad can be a rewarding experience. If you have specific questions about programs or application processes, feel free to ask!

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: “So what is the difference when I'm going to another country to study and having a work now, having an internship working now? I don't know what to choose.”

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Choosing between studying abroad and starting work now depends on your personal goals. Here are some differences to consider: Studying Abroad: Education: You’ll gain specialized knowledge and potentially earn an advanced degree. Networking: You’ll build an international network and gain global perspectives. Experience: Living in a new country can enhance personal growth and adaptability. Starting Work Now: Practical Experience: You’ll gain hands-on experience in your field, which can be valuable for your career development. Financial Independence: Earning a salary can help you achieve financial independence and save for future plans. Career Start: Early work experience can help you build a strong foundation for your career. Think about what aligns best with your long-term goals and current situation. Feel free to ask if you need more guidance!

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: “So, what should I do now if I want to apply for an internship next year?”

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: If you’re planning to apply for a master’s program next year, here are some steps you can take to prepare: Research Programs: Look into universities and programs that interest you. Consider their curriculum, faculty, and admission requirements. Standardized Tests: Check if the programs require tests like the GRE or GMAT. Start preparing for them. Documents: Gather necessary documents like transcripts, letters of recommendation, and a statement of purpose. Finances: Plan your budget and explore scholarships or financial aid options. Skill Development: Enhance relevant skills, such as language proficiency or technical skills, depending on your field. Networking: Connect with alumni or current students from the programs to get insights. Taking these steps can help you prepare effectively for your master’s application. If you need more specific guidance, feel free to ask!

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: “How can I stay... So, yeah, I'm really thankful that you help me a lot, that you give me a lot of information about what can I choose. But how can I stay motivated if I'm not sure about my goals yet?”

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: It’s completely normal to feel uncertain about your goals sometimes. Here are a few tips to stay motivated: Set Small Goals: Break your larger goals into smaller, manageable tasks. Celebrate each accomplishment. Stay Curious: Keep exploring different aspects of your field. Attend workshops, read articles, or connect with professionals. Reflect: Take time to reflect on what excites you and what values are important to you. Seek Support: Talk to mentors, advisors, or peers. Sometimes, discussing your thoughts can provide clarity. Stay Flexible: It’s okay if your goals change over time. Adapt and embrace new opportunities as they come. Remember, it’s a journey, and you’re not alone. You’ve got this!

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: “Yeah, thank you so much about your Motivating me like and who else I can talk to for careers on studying advice at university I can have more motivation and have more information and have more People like around me and they can Encourage me for doing For for dealing with my career with my study”

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: It’s great that you’re seeking support and guidance. Here are some people you can talk to at your university: Career Services Office: They offer career counseling, resume reviews, and job search assistance. Academic Advisors: They can help you plan your courses and provide guidance on aligning your studies with your career goals. Professors and Mentors: They can offer advice based on their own experiences and connections in the field. Student Organizations: Joining clubs related to your field can provide networking opportunities and support from peers. Alumni Network: Alumni can share their career paths and offer mentorship. These resources can provide valuable guidance and encouragement. Don’t hesitate to reach out to them!

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: “The last thing I want to ask is, like, can I book another meeting, another follow-up meeting with you after I decide what I want to choose?”

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Of course! You can schedule a follow-up meeting with me by contacting the university’s career services office or academic advising center. You can usually do this through their website or by calling their office. Just let them know your availability, and we can arrange a time that works for you. Feel free to reach out anytime you need guidance or support. I’m here to help!
